hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'gratia' has the same form but would need a preposition or different context — here it is the subject of 'tollit'
hints.Vocative: This is a philosophical statement, not an address — 'gratia' is nominative subject
hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'gratiae' — 'gratia' is nominative here

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'gratia' with -a ending is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'gratia, gratiae' is standard 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'gratia' is 1st declension

hints.to lift up: 'tollere' can mean 'to lift up', but in this theological context with 'non' it means 'to take away / destroy'
hints.to endure: 'to endure' is 'tolerare' — do not confuse with 'tollere'

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'sustulit' — 'tollit' is present indicative
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'tollat' — 'tollit' with -it is indicative
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'tollet' — 'tollit' is present indicative

hints.tolero, tolerare, toleravi, toleratum: These are the parts of 'tolerare' (to endure) — a different verb
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry) — note 'tollere' has its own irregular perfect 'sustuli'
hints.attollo, attollere: This is a compound of 'tollere' (to raise up) — but the passage uses simple 'tollere'

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'natura' — 'naturam' with -am is accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'natura' — 'naturam' is accusative, object of 'tollit'

hints.2nd: 2nd declension accusative ends in -um — 'naturam' with -am is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension accusative ends in -em — 'naturam' is 1st declension
hints.5th: 5th declension accusative ends in -em — 'naturam' with -am is 1st declension

hints.to make: 'to make' is 'facere' — 'perficit' comes from 'perficere' (per + facere = to complete)
hints.to perceive: 'to perceive' is 'percipere' — do not confuse with 'perficere'
hints.to accomplish thoroughly: Close but 'perficere' specifically means 'to perfect / bring to completion'

hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'perfecit' — 'perficit' is present indicative
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'perficiat' — 'perficit' is indicative
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'perficiet' — 'perficit' is present indicative

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the parts of 'facere' (to make) — the simple verb; 'perficere' is its compound
hints.percipio, percipere, percepi, perceptum: These are the parts of 'percipere' (to perceive) — a per + capere compound
hints.proficio, proficere, profeci, profectum: These are the parts of 'proficere' (to advance) — a different compound of 'facere'

translation: Grace does not destroy nature but perfects it.
